items.prompt_md: Why do two anagrams (e.g. `"listen"` and `"silent"`) always end up with identical letter-frequency signatures?
options: Because rearranging letters never changes how many times each letter appears
Because both strings have the same length
Because anagrams are always sorted the same way
Because hash functions map anagrams to the same string
items.answer: Because rearranging letters never changes how many times each letter appears
items.explanation_md: "Anagram" just means rearrangement — shuffling letters changes their order but not their multiset, so the per-letter counts (the signature) are identical no matter how the letters are scrambled.

items.prompt_md: Building a 26-slot frequency array for a single string of length `k` costs how much time?
items.answer: O(k)
items.explanation_md: Each of the `k` characters triggers exactly one array increment — a constant-time operation — so the total work is linear in the string's length.

items.prompt_md: When the alphabet is fixed and small (26 lowercase letters), why might a plain fixed-size array beat a general hash map for counting?
options: Array indexing is O(1) with no hashing overhead, and the size is known in advance
Arrays can grow dynamically while hash maps cannot
items.answer: Array indexing is O(1) with no hashing overhead, and the size is known in advance
items.explanation_md: Since the domain (26 letters) is small and known ahead of time, each letter maps directly to an index — same O(1) access as a hash map, but without the hashing and bucketing machinery a general-purpose map needs.

items.prompt_md: What's the space cost of the frequency array itself, in terms of the alphabet size `k` (not the input length `n`)?
items.answer: O(k)
items.explanation_md: The array holds one counter per possible letter, so its size is proportional to the alphabet size `k`, independent of how many characters `n` are actually processed.

items.prompt_md: Code writes `counts[ch] += 1` on a plain `dict` without initializing `counts[ch]` first. What happens the first time a new character is seen?
options: KeyError, because there's no default value to increment
It appends ch to a list instead
items.answer: KeyError, because there's no default value to increment
items.explanation_md: A plain `dict` has no default value, so `+=` first tries to read `counts[ch]`, which doesn't exist yet — that lookup raises `KeyError` before the increment can happen. `defaultdict(int)` or `Counter` sidesteps this by supplying an implicit zero.

items.prompt_md: Why must a frequency signature be a tuple (or another hashable structure) rather than a plain list when it's used as a dict key?
options: Dict keys must be hashable, and Python lists are mutable and unhashable
Tuples take less memory than lists
items.answer: Dict keys must be hashable, and Python lists are mutable and unhashable
items.explanation_md: Python requires dict keys to be hashable so their hash can be computed once and reused for bucket lookup. Lists are mutable, so Python disallows hashing them; tuples are immutable, so they work as keys.

items.prompt_md: Comparing two frequency structures (arrays or dicts over the same fixed alphabet) for equality costs how much, in terms of alphabet size `k`?
items.answer: O(k)
items.explanation_md: The equality check walks each of the `k` slots once to confirm the counts match, so it's linear in the alphabet size.

items.prompt_md: "eat" and "tea" are compared using a sorted-string key instead of a count array. What's the extra cost per string compared to counting?
options: Sorting costs O(k log k) per string versus O(k) for counting
Sorting is O(k) too, no difference
Sorting requires a hash map internally
items.answer: Sorting costs O(k log k) per string versus O(k) for counting
items.explanation_md: Sorting a string of length `k` costs O(k log k), while tallying letters into a count array only costs O(k) — same correctness, but counting is asymptotically cheaper as strings get longer.

items.prompt_md: A function counts word frequencies with `Counter`, then reports the "least frequent" word via `min(counts, key=counts.get)`. What's a subtle risk here?
options: Ties aren't resolved deterministically — any of several equally-rare words could be returned
Counter cannot be used with min()
This always returns the most frequent word
items.answer: Ties aren't resolved deterministically — any of several equally-rare words could be returned
items.explanation_md: When several words share the lowest count, `min` just returns the first one it encounters in iteration order — it isn't wrong, but it's easy to assume there's a single "the" least frequent word when several are tied.

items.prompt_md: For an array of `n` elements built into a frequency map where values can repeat arbitrarily (not a small bounded alphabet), what's the worst-case space complexity?
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: If every element is distinct, the frequency map ends up with `n` separate keys, so space grows linearly with input size in the worst case — unlike the fixed O(26) array used for bounded alphabets.

items.prompt_md: Checking "are these two strings anagrams" with one array, incrementing for string A and decrementing for string B, then checking "all zero" — why does this work in a single combined pass?
options: Every letter that appears equally often in both strings cancels back to zero; any imbalance leaves a nonzero slot
It's not possible to build two separate structures
items.answer: Every letter that appears equally often in both strings cancels back to zero; any imbalance leaves a nonzero slot
items.explanation_md: Walking string A and incrementing, then walking string B and decrementing over the same array, makes every letter cancel out exactly when the strings are true anagrams — replacing a separate comparison pass, at the same overall O(k) cost.

items.prompt_md: If the input could contain uppercase letters, digits, or Unicode characters instead of just lowercase a-z, what breaks about `counts[ord(ch) - ord('a')]`?
options: The index could land outside the array's 26 slots (or go negative), causing an out-of-range error
Nothing breaks, ord() handles all characters the same way
It would only break for digits, not uppercase letters
It would silently produce the correct answer anyway
items.answer: The index could land outside the array's 26 slots (or go negative), causing an out-of-range error
items.explanation_md: `ord(ch) - ord('a')` only maps cleanly onto 0-25 for lowercase a-z. Any other character produces an index outside the array's bounds, so the fix is a bigger array, a per-case offset, or falling back to a general hash map keyed on the character itself.

items.prompt_md: You're given a huge list of words and need to bucket them so that every anagram of a word ends up in the same group as every other anagram of it.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: Grouping by composition, not order, across many items is the frequency-map trigger — one counting pass per word plus a dict keyed on the resulting signature groups everything in O(n·k), with no pairwise work.

items.prompt_md: Given two strings, you need a yes/no answer to whether one is a rearrangement of the other.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: A single pair only needs one comparison, but the underlying question is still "do the letter counts match" — counting each string into an array (or dict) and comparing is the direct, linear-time route.

items.prompt_md: You need to find which word appears most often across a large block of text.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: "How many times does each item occur" is exactly what a frequency map answers in one linear pass — no need to sort or search once counts are tracked directly.

items.prompt_md: A string can be rearranged into a palindrome only if at most one character has an odd count. You need to check this condition for a given string.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: The condition is stated directly in terms of character counts, so building a frequency count and checking how many entries are odd is the natural, linear-time approach.

items.prompt_md: Given an array, determine whether any value shows up more than once.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: Detecting repeats is a counting question — a hash map (or set) tracking how many times each value has been seen catches a duplicate the moment its count exceeds one, in a single linear pass.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the first character in a string that does not repeat anywhere else in it.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: "Does not repeat" is a property of a character's count being exactly one — tally every character's frequency first, then scan in original order for the first count-of-one.

items.prompt_md: Given two arrays, decide whether they contain exactly the same elements the same number of times, ignoring order.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: "Same elements, same multiplicities, order doesn't matter" is a multiset-equality question — count each array into a frequency map and compare the two maps directly.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum number of single-character deletions to turn one string into an anagram of another.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: The deletion count falls straight out of the per-character count difference between the two strings — tally both, then sum the absolute differences per character.

items.prompt_md: You're building a word game and need to know, for any candidate word, whether it can be spelled using the letters in a fixed hand of tiles (each tile used at most once).
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: "Can this be built from a fixed pool of letters" is a per-letter supply check — count the tiles once, then compare each candidate word's own letter counts against that supply.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of numbers, output them sorted by how often each one appears, from most frequent to least frequent.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: The ordering criterion is frequency itself, so the first step is always the same — build a count for every value — before any sorting or bucketing happens on top of those counts.

items.prompt_md: You need to check, across a huge batch of review strings, which ones are exact letter-for-letter rearrangements of a single target word.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: Checking many candidates against one fixed target's letter composition is a repeated count-comparison — count the target once, then count (or incrementally check) each candidate against it in linear time per string.

items.prompt_md: Given a list where one value occurs strictly more often than any other, you need to identify that value.
options: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
Two pointers on a sorted array
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map / frequency array
items.explanation_md: Identifying "which value occurs most" is directly answered by counting occurrences of each value and reading off the max — no ordering or windowing needed.
